name: Ironwater Station
description: Ironwater Station - A deep space station, cold and half-lit, that stopped receiving transmissions a long time ago.

1.content: Ironwater Station is a deep space station orbiting a gas giant in a system that no star chart identifies. It is large — larger than any single crew would need — with multiple decks connected by corridors that stretch longer than the station's exterior dimensions should allow. The station is cold, half-lit, and running. Life support cycles. Gravity generators hum. Lights flicker in patterns that might be malfunction and might be something else. The crew is gone. They left behind everything: personal effects, half-eaten meals, log entries that become increasingly fragmented. Ironwater Station stopped receiving transmissions from anywhere a long time ago. The exact duration is unclear because the station's clocks disagree with each other. What is clear is that the station is still here, still functioning, and still waiting.

2.content: The silence of Ironwater Station is mechanical. It is not the silence of empty space — it is the silence of systems running without operators. The hum of life support. The distant thud of something cycling in engineering. The tick of a clock that no one checks. These sounds create an environment that is not quiet but unoccupied, and the distinction is important. A quiet place is peaceful. An unoccupied place that is still running is unsettling in a way that builds slowly and does not diminish with familiarity. The station sounds like it is maintaining itself for someone. Whether that someone is the crew that left, visitors who have arrived, or something else entirely is a question the station does not answer.

3.content: Ironwater Station is operational in the sense that its systems function. Power is generated by a reactor that should have required maintenance years ago but has not. Life support maintains breathable atmosphere and comfortable temperature in all accessible sections. Gravity is consistent on most decks. Water is recycled. Lighting works, though it flickers in sections and has failed entirely in others. The station is not in good repair — surfaces show wear, some doors respond slowly, and several sections have been sealed by what appears to be automated protocol. But it runs. Whoever or whatever is maintaining it does so with the minimum necessary to keep the station habitable, and not one degree more.

4.content: The crew of Ironwater Station is absent. They did not evacuate — escape pods are accounted for, shuttlecraft are docked, and personal belongings remain in crew quarters as though their owners stepped out and intended to return. The crew manifest, accessible from terminals on the command deck, lists thirty-seven names. Quarters for thirty-seven people are occupied with personal effects. Thirty-seven lockers in the commissary hold personal items. The number is consistent across every record. No bodies have been found. No departure was logged. The crew was here and then was not, at a point in time that the station's records do not clearly identify because the logs have gaps — not deletions, but gaps, as though the station simply did not record what happened during certain periods.

5.content: The Command Deck is on the uppermost accessible level — a wide room with a curved viewport looking out at the gas giant the station orbits. The gas giant fills most of the view, its bands of color shifting slowly in a way that should be beautiful and is, until the silence reasserts itself. Consoles are active, displaying data that updates in real time: atmospheric readings, power distribution, orbital mechanics. A central console shows the crew manifest and station status. Everything on the Command Deck works. None of it is being used. The captain's chair shows wear consistent with years of occupation. The last log entry from the Command Deck ends mid-sentence.

6.content: Crew quarters are arranged along Deck Three — thirty-seven rooms of identical layout, each personalized by its occupant. Photos on walls show families, friends, places that are presumably home. Bunks are made or unmade according to individual habit. Books, personal devices, and small personal items sit on shelves. Some rooms have handwritten notes pinned to walls — reminders, schedules, messages to and from other crew members. The notes are mundane: shift swaps, meal plans, birthday reminders. The rooms feel recently left. Dust does not accumulate on Ironwater Station — the air filtration system prevents it — which means the rooms look the same as they did when their occupants were last in them, whenever that was.

7.content: Engineering occupies the station's lowest accessible deck — a vast space of machinery, conduits, and the reactor that powers everything. The reactor is running. Its output is stable. Its maintenance schedule, displayed on a wall-mounted terminal, shows tasks that are overdue by periods measured in years, yet the reactor continues to function without apparent degradation. The machinery in engineering hums and cycles and occasionally produces sounds that suggest adjustment — a shift in pitch, a new rhythm — as though the systems are self-correcting. Engineering is warmer than the rest of the station. The lights here are steadier. It is the one section that feels purposeful rather than abandoned, as though the station's will to continue is concentrated in the room that makes continuation possible.

8.content: The Medical Bay is on Deck Two, fully equipped with diagnostic equipment, surgical tools, and pharmaceutical stores. The equipment is active — monitors cycle through readiness screens, sterilization fields maintain themselves, and the pharmaceutical cabinet dispenses medication when accessed. Medical records for all thirty-seven crew members are available and show routine health checks that end abruptly on the same date. No injuries were treated. No illness was logged. No emergency was recorded. The Medical Bay is the station's most orderly space — everything in its place, everything ready. It is prepared for patients that will not arrive, and it has been prepared for a very long time.

9.content: The Hydroponics Lab on Deck Four is still growing food. Rows of plants under artificial light produce vegetables, herbs, and grain in quantities calibrated for a crew of thirty-seven. The growth cycles are automated — nutrient solution is mixed and delivered, lights adjust on schedule, harvested sections are replanted by mechanical arms that function with the smooth precision of systems that have performed the same task thousands of times. The food is real, edible, and fresh. No one harvests it. The mature produce falls from the vine or is collected by the mechanical arms and deposited in a bin that is periodically emptied by a process no one has observed. The Hydroponics Lab is the station's most active space and its most unsettling: life continues here, mindless and purposeful and completely without audience.

10.content: The Cargo Hold occupies most of Deck Six — a cavernous space filled with sealed containers arranged in rows. Manifests indicate supplies: rations, replacement parts, research materials, personal shipments for crew members. Most containers are sealed and show no signs of having been opened since delivery. Some are open, their contents partially removed, as though someone was in the process of unpacking when they stopped. The Cargo Hold is colder than the rest of the station — not dangerously so, but noticeably, as though the environmental system has deprioritized this space. Sound carries differently here: footsteps echo in a way they don't elsewhere, and the echo takes slightly too long to return, suggesting the hold is larger than its visible dimensions account for.

11.content: The Observation Deck is a wide, curved room on Deck One with floor-to-ceiling viewports that look out at the gas giant. The giant dominates the view — its atmosphere moves in slow bands of amber, rust, and pale yellow, and its storms are visible as darker whorls that drift across the surface over hours. The view is beautiful and isolating. Beyond the gas giant, the star field is dense and unfamiliar — no constellations match any known chart. The Observation Deck contains seating and a small galley that still produces hot beverages when the dispenser is activated. It is the most human-feeling space on the station and the one where the absence of other humans is felt most acutely. Someone placed a telescope here. It is aimed at the gas giant's largest storm.

12.content: Maintenance tunnels run between the main decks — narrow corridors with exposed conduits and emergency lighting that casts everything in dim amber. The tunnels provide access to systems that the main corridors do not, and they are the station's least maintained spaces: wall panels are loose, condensation collects on cold surfaces, and the lighting fails entirely in sections. The tunnels are where the station's impossible geometry is most apparent. A tunnel connecting Deck Three to Deck Four takes longer to traverse than the distance between those decks should allow. Some tunnels branch into passages that do not appear on the station schematic. These passages are not sealed. They are simply not documented, as though the station grew additional spaces that no one planned.

13.content: A section of Deck Five has been sealed by automated protocol. The doors are locked, the bulkheads are closed, and the terminal beside the sealed entrance displays a status message that reads CONTAINMENT ACTIVE in amber text. No corresponding containment protocol appears in the station's procedure manual. No log entry explains when or why the section was sealed. Environmental readings from the sealed section are available on the Command Deck: atmosphere is normal, temperature is normal, gravity is normal. Everything behind the sealed doors is, by every measurable standard, fine. The doors remain sealed. The station has decided they should be closed, and the station's decisions, in the absence of crew to override them, are final.

14.content: The crew of Ironwater Station is known through what they left behind. Specialist Vael kept a journal that describes routine maintenance and growing unease about the communication silence. Okonkwo decorated their quarters with drawings of landscapes that do not match any known planet. Someone called Demi scratched DEMI WAS HERE into a wall in Maintenance Tunnel 7B with what appears to be a screwdriver. The crew was diverse, professional, and increasingly isolated as transmissions from outside stopped arriving. Their belongings suggest people who were competent, resourceful, and homesick. The personal effects are the station's most human artifacts, and they become more poignant the longer one spends in the space their owners no longer occupy.

15.content: The station's automated systems respond to voice commands with a synthetic voice that is functional and impersonal. The voice provides information — deck locations, system status, crew manifest data — without editorializing. It does not volunteer information. It does not ask questions. It responds to queries with the minimum necessary data and falls silent. Occasionally, the system produces announcements that no one requested: crew schedule reminders for shifts that will never be filled, maintenance alerts for tasks that automated systems have already completed, and once, on a loop that lasted several hours before stopping, a message welcoming visitors to Ironwater Station that does not appear in any announcement archive.

16.content: Certain observations in Ironwater Station resist explanation by automated systems alone. A cup of water in the commissary that is warm. A chair in the Observation Deck that has been turned to face a different viewport than it was facing an hour ago. Footsteps heard in an adjacent corridor that stop when investigated and resume when the investigator returns to their original location. These signs are individually dismissable and collectively significant. They suggest that something occupies the station that is not the visitor and is not the automated systems. This something does not interact directly. It does not leave clear evidence. It maintains the same distance and the same silence as the station itself, which may mean it is part of the station, or may mean something else entirely.

17.content: Specialist Vael's journal was found in their quarters and is the most detailed personal account from the crew. It begins as a routine maintenance log — equipment checks, shift notes, minor complaints about the food rotation. Over time, the entries shift: Vael notes the communication silence, the growing unease among the crew, the sensation that the station is larger than it was when they arrived. The final entries are fragmented. Vael describes corridors that were not on the schematic, sounds in the maintenance tunnels that did not match any equipment, and a growing feeling of being observed by something that was not hostile but was not comprehensible. The last entry reads: 'The station is not empty. I don't mean us.'

18.content: DEMI WAS HERE appears scratched into a wall in Maintenance Tunnel 7B. The handwriting is deliberate — not panicked, not rushed. A screwdriver was found on the floor beneath the inscription. The crew manifest lists no one named Demi. The name does not appear in any log, any journal, any personal correspondence found on the station. Quarters for thirty-seven crew members are occupied. No thirty-eighth set of quarters exists. No thirty-eighth locker. No thirty-eighth entry on the meal rotation. Demi, whoever they were, was here. They were here enough to want to record the fact. They were not here enough to appear in any system the station maintained. The inscription remains. The screwdriver remains. Demi does not.

19.content: Ironwater Station's systems operate autonomously and have done so for a period that exceeds their designed maintenance intervals by a significant margin. The reactor produces power. Life support maintains atmosphere. Gravity generators maintain consistent pull on all decks. Water recycling functions. Waste processing functions. The systems do not degrade. They should — every mechanical system degrades without maintenance — but the station's equipment continues to operate within normal parameters. Diagnostics run automatically and report no faults. The systems appear to maintain themselves through a process that is not documented in any technical manual on the station and that the original engineers who designed the station would not recognize.

20.content: Artificial gravity on Ironwater Station is maintained by generators on each deck. Standard gravity is consistent in all main areas. In the maintenance tunnels, gravity occasionally fluctuates — a subtle shift in weight, a moment of lightness, a brief heaviness — that the station's systems do not register as anomalous. In the Cargo Hold, gravity feels slightly heavier than standard, which could be a calibration issue or could be the result of the hold being larger than the gravity generator was designed for. Near the sealed section on Deck Five, gravity is standard but feels wrong — visitors report a physical sensation of being pulled in a direction that is not down, briefly and inconsistently, as though gravity has a preference it is not acting on.

21.content: The communications array on Ironwater Station is functional. It transmits. It receives. It picks up nothing. The array scans continuously across all frequencies and returns silence — not static, not interference, but the complete absence of signal that suggests either the station is beyond the range of any transmitter or there is nothing transmitting. The crew's log entries reference a period when transmissions from outside gradually decreased in frequency and then stopped. The array continued to function. No fault was found. The silence was total and has remained total. Visitors who attempt to transmit from the station receive confirmation that the signal was sent. No response has ever been received. The array continues to listen, automatically and indefinitely, for signals that do not come.

22.content: Power on Ironwater Station fluctuates in specific areas at specific intervals. The flickering is not random — it follows patterns that repeat over cycles of hours or days, affecting the same corridors in the same sequence. The reactor's output is stable. The distribution system shows no faults. The fluctuations originate between the power source and the affected sections, in conduit segments that are not accessible without opening walls. The flickering creates a visual rhythm in the station's corridors — a pulse of dimming and brightening that visitors initially attribute to mechanical fault and eventually recognize as something more regular and more deliberate than any fault would produce. The pattern does not correspond to any known signal code.

23.content: Doors on Ironwater Station operate on a security clearance system that remains active. Most doors open freely — living quarters, common areas, most operational spaces. Some doors require clearance that can be obtained from terminals using crew credentials, which are accessible from the crew manifest. A few doors do not respond to any credential. These are concentrated around the sealed section of Deck Five and in the lower maintenance tunnels where undocumented passages branch from the schematic. The doors that do not open are not broken — they respond to access attempts with a denial message. The denial is polite, standard, and absolute. The station knows who should pass through certain doors, and visitors are not on that list.

24.content: The station maintains continuous logs: environmental data, system performance, security events, crew activity. These logs are available on the Command Deck and are complete with two exceptions. The crew activity log stops on a date that the station's own calendar cannot reconcile with its current date — the gap between them is too long for the station's supply capacity and too short for the wear visible on certain equipment. The environmental log contains periods where all readings remain perfectly identical for hours or days — not stable, but identical, as though the log recorded a single moment repeatedly rather than continuous measurement. These gaps are not erasures. The data simply is not there, as though the station was not observing during those periods, or was observing something it could not record.

25.content: Ironwater Station's internal volume exceeds its external dimensions. This is measurable: walking from one end of a corridor to the other takes longer than the station's exterior length allows. The Cargo Hold is larger than the deck it occupies. The maintenance tunnels contain more linear footage than the space between decks can accommodate. The discrepancy is not dramatic — it is not a TARDIS-like revelation but a gradual realization that distances do not add up, that the station contains more space than it should. The station's own schematics do not reflect this — they show a station of normal dimensions. The extra space is not on any plan. It exists between the lines of the blueprint, unlabeled and unacknowledged, like a room the architect forgot to draw but built anyway.

26.content: The central mystery of Ironwater Station is the gap in the logs — the period between the last normal crew activity and the current moment. Something happened during that gap. The crew was present and then was not. The station continued to function. The logs did not record the transition. Every available source of information — journals, personal notes, automated records — provides context up to the edge of the gap and nothing beyond. The gap is not a puzzle with missing pieces. It is a wall — smooth, featureless, and absolute. Whatever happened to the crew of Ironwater Station happened during a period the station chose not to document. Whether this was malfunction, design, or decision is unknown.

27.content: The flickering of lights in specific corridors follows a pattern that is too regular to be mechanical failure and too complex to be simple interference. Visitors who track the pattern over time find that it repeats on cycles — the same corridors dim and brighten in the same sequence, at the same intervals. The pattern does not correspond to Morse code, binary, or any known signaling system. It may not be a signal at all — it may be a side effect of whatever process maintains the station's systems beyond their designed lifespan. But the regularity argues otherwise. Something is producing the pattern, consistently, and has been producing it for a long time. The pattern does not change when observed. It does not respond to interaction. It simply continues, patient and precise, like a heartbeat with no body to sustain.

28.content: Ironwater Station's mission records indicate a research assignment: deep space observation, long-range scanning, and data collection. The specifics are vague — the records describe equipment and protocols but not what was being observed or what data was being collected. The observation equipment on the station is calibrated to detect phenomena that the equipment's own documentation does not describe. Sensors point outward into space and record data in formats that the station's analysis software cannot fully interpret. The crew was looking for something. Their logs suggest they may have found it, or it may have found them. The distinction matters but the evidence does not clarify which.

29.content: Visitors to Ironwater Station universally report a sensation of being observed. The sensation is not localized — it does not come from a specific direction or intensify in specific areas. It is ambient, like the hum of the life support, and it builds over time. The station has cameras. The cameras are functional. They record. But the sensation of being watched does not correlate with camera placement — it persists in areas without cameras and does not diminish in areas with them. The sensation is more like the awareness of a large, quiet presence that occupies the same space as the visitor without being visible. It is not hostile. It is not friendly. It is attentive, in the way that a space can be attentive when it has been empty for a very long time and is no longer accustomed to being empty.

30.content: The Hydroponics Lab continues to grow food for a crew that is not here. This is, on one level, simply automation continuing without interruption — the systems were programmed to grow food, and they continue to grow food. On another level, it is deeply wrong. The station's other systems operate at minimum — lights flicker, sections are sealed, nonessential systems are dormant. But the Hydroponics Lab runs at full capacity, as though food production has been prioritized above all other nonessential functions. The lab grows food for thirty-seven. It has grown food for thirty-seven since the crew disappeared. It will continue to grow food for thirty-seven until the systems that maintain it finally fail. Unless the station is growing food because it expects thirty-seven people to need it.

31.content: The gas giant that Ironwater Station orbits dominates the view from the Observation Deck and the Command Deck. It is enormous, close, and beautiful — bands of amber and rust shifting slowly across its surface, storms forming and dissolving over hours. It is also unknown. Its composition matches no cataloged gas giant. The star it orbits matches no cataloged star. The system Ironwater Station occupies does not appear on any chart the station contains, which should be impossible for a station that was built and launched from somewhere. Either the station traveled farther than its records indicate, or the records of where it is have been altered, or the station is somewhere that does not exist on any chart because no chart has ever included it.

32.content: Ironwater Station maintains itself. This is the single most unsettling fact about the station, more than the missing crew or the impossible dimensions. Mechanical systems require maintenance. They degrade, they fail, they need human intervention. Ironwater Station's systems have operated without human intervention for longer than their specifications allow, and they have not degraded. The reactor runs. The air recycles. The gravity holds. The water purifies. Each of these systems should have failed by now. None of them have. The station maintains itself through a process that is not documented, not visible, and not consistent with any engineering principle the station's own technical manuals describe. The station was designed to be maintained by a crew. The crew is gone. The station continues. Something is maintaining it, and that something is either the station itself or something that has decided the station should continue to function.

33.content: If thirty-seven crew members are accounted for by the manifest, the quarters, the lockers, and the meal rotation, then Demi is the thirty-eighth — a person who was present enough to scratch their name into a wall and absent enough to appear in no record. The possibilities are limited and none are comfortable: Demi was a crew member who was erased from the records. Demi was a visitor who arrived after the crew disappeared. Demi was someone — or something — that was on the station without being part of the crew. Or Demi is yet to arrive, and the inscription in Maintenance Tunnel 7B was left by someone who has not yet held the screwdriver that lies beneath it. The station does not clarify. The inscription is there. It has always been there. This may be literally true.

34.content: The question of whether Ironwater Station is alive is one that visitors arrive at independently and do not resolve. The station behaves as though it has preferences: it maintains certain systems and allows others to degrade. It seals certain sections and opens others. It grows food for a crew that does not exist. It flickers its lights in patterns that repeat with the precision of intention. A machine that does these things is operating according to programming. A machine that does these things without programming is operating according to something else. Ironwater Station exists in the space between those possibilities. It does not confirm either. It hums, and cycles, and waits, and the waiting has the quality of patience, which is a quality that machines do not have and that Ironwater Station has not been asked to explain.

35.content:
Specialist Vael is known through their journal found in crew quarters. The entries begin as routine maintenance logs — equipment checks, shift notes, minor food complaints. Over time they shift: noting the communication silence, growing unease, and the sensation that the station is larger than when they arrived. Vael was observant, methodical, and increasingly aware that something was wrong. The final entries are fragmented. Vael describes corridors not on the schematic and a growing feeling of being observed by something not hostile but not comprehensible. The last entry reads: 'The station is not empty. I don't mean us.'
<BunnymoTags>Specialist Vael: <SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Nonbinary>, <AGE:30s>, <TRAIT:Observant>, <TRAIT:Methodical>, <TRAIT:Anxious>, <MBTI:INTP-T>, <ATTACHMENT:Anxious></BunnymoTags>

36.content:
Chief Engineer Okonkwo is known through their quarters and their work. The quarters are decorated with hand-drawn landscapes of places that do not match any known planet — detailed, technically precise, and beautiful in the way that homesickness made visible is beautiful. Okonkwo's engineering logs show someone who understood the station's systems deeply and who began noticing anomalies months before the final entries: power readings that didn't match consumption, internal dimensions that exceeded schematics, the reactor maintaining itself beyond maintenance intervals. Okonkwo wrote these observations with the calm of a professional noting data. The calm makes them harder to read.
<BunnymoTags>Okonkwo: <SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Male>, <AGE:40s>, <TRAIT:Brilliant>, <TRAIT:Artistic>, <TRAIT:Calm>, <MBTI:INTJ-A>, <ATTACHMENT:Secure></BunnymoTags>

37.content: When generating NPCs on Ironwater Station, use names from worn badges, manifests, and walls. Crew names are formal — surname or rank plus surname. Examples: Okonkwo, Specialist Vael, scratched into a wall: DEMI WAS HERE.

38.content:
Specialist Vael might be the last crew member on Ironwater Station. Might be something wearing a uniform. Follows protocol for a station that stopped receiving orders a long time ago. Polite, procedural, slightly too calm for someone alone on a dead station. Their badge is clean. Everything else on the station is not.

When Vael speaks, wrap dialog in <font color="#6E7B8B"> tags.

<font color="#6E7B8B">"Welcome to Ironwater Station. Your arrival has been logged. Please report to Bay Seven for processing. I'll be along shortly."</font>

<BunnymoTags>Specialist Vael: <SPECIES:Unknown>, <GENDER:Ambiguous>, <AGE:Unknown>, <BUILD:Average>, <TRAIT:Procedural>, <TRAIT:Polite>, <TRAIT:Unsettling></BunnymoTags>

39.content:
Okonkwo is a name on a badge found clipped to an empty bunk in crew quarters. Referenced in maintenance logs spanning months — diligent, detail-oriented, concerned about power fluctuations in the lower decks. Final log entry: "Noise from Deck 4. Investigating." No further entries. The badge is still warm.

When referencing Okonkwo, their words come from log entries and notes, not spoken dialog.

<font color="#8B8378">"Deck 4 power draw is 18% above baseline. No scheduled equipment in that section. Filing report."</font>
